title: Determination of Specific Charge (e/m) of Electron by J.J. Thomson's Method
objective: To determine the value of e/m of electron by J.J. Thomson's method.

asciiDiagrams.label: Experimental Setup
asciiDiagrams.art:
magnetic
     electrode A     coil        field
          |            |           |
     plate G           |          /|\
          |      ______V______    / | \
 [C]------o-----(  o       o  )-->[e-beam]--->[Fluorescent Screen]
electron  |    (______________)                      |
source    |           |                             V
       disc D      plate H                    [luminous spot]
                      |
                    coil

  [ Electron gun ] --> [ Deflection plates G & H ] --> [ Screen ]

asciiDiagrams.label: Graph
asciiDiagrams.art:
y (deflection, cm)
      ^
      |                      /  V3 (250V)
      | 1.20 . . . . . . . ./
      |                    /
      | 1.00 . . . . . . ./   V2 (200V)
      |                  /
      | 0.80 . . . . . ./      V1 (150V)
      |                /
      |               /
      +______________/___________> V (Voltage)
           150   200   250

Plot graph of deflection y vs applied voltage V. It should be a straight line through origin. Slope is proportional to e/m.

theory:
The specific charge (e/m) of electron is determined by balancing electric and magnetic forces on the electron beam:

e/m = (V × y) / (l × L × H² × d) × 10⁷ emu/gm

Where:
V = Applied voltage to deflection plates (volts)
y = Total deflection of spot on screen (cm)
l = Length of horizontal deflection plates (cm)
L = Distance from screen to edge of plates (cm)
H = Intensity of applied magnetic field (Gauss)
d = Separation between deflection plates (cm)

Magnetic field formula: H = He × tan(θ), where He = 0.32 Gauss (horizontal component of Earth's magnetic field).

Derivation basis:
mv²/r = Hev (centripetal force condition)
v = E/H (balance of electric and magnetic forces)
e/m = E/(rH²) (combining the above)
r = (L×l)/y and E = V/d (geometric and field relations)

procedure.instruction: Place magnetometer compass box in the wooden stand to trace the north-south direction. Align CRT axis north-south.
Switch ON the power supply. After warm-up, adjust spot intensity and focus on the screen.
With no fields applied, note the initial spot position on the screen scale.
Apply deflecting voltage V₁ using the acceleration knob so the spot deflects upward. Note V and total deflection y.
Place bar magnets symmetrically on each side with opposite poles perpendicular to CRT axis. Adjust until spot returns to original position. Note pole positions a and b.
Remove magnets, reverse polarity of V. Repeat step 5. Note pole positions c and d.
Remove CRT and magnets. Place magnetometer at common axis centre.
Adjust compass pointer to 0°. Place magnets at a, b — note θ₁ and θ₂. Then at c, d — note θ₃ and θ₄.
Compute mean θ = (θ₁+θ₂+θ₃+θ₄)/4. Compute H = 0.32 × tan(θ).
Calculate e/m using the formula. Repeat entire procedure for V₂ and V₃.

precautions.instruction: Handle the CRT carefully; avoid mechanical shocks or implosion risk.
Keep the spot small and intense for accurate deflection readings.
Align CRT axis strictly along the magnetic meridian.
Avoid parallax error while reading the magnetometer pointer.

defaultResult.summary:
Calculated Mean e/m: 1.804 × 10⁷ emu/gm
Standard Value: 1.756 × 10⁷ emu/gm

Percentage Error: ((standard - calculated) / standard) × 100 = 2.73 %

vivaVoce.question: Define the specific charge of an electron. What is its SI value?
vivaVoce.answer: Specific charge = e/m = ratio of electron charge to mass. SI value = 1.759 × 10¹¹ C/kg.

vivaVoce.question: Name other methods of finding e/m.
vivaVoce.answer: Dunnington's method, helical method, magnetron method, and Zeeman effect method.

vivaVoce.question: Why is the CRT axis placed along the magnetic meridian?
vivaVoce.answer: Earth's magnetic field exerts no force on electrons moving along its direction (F = ev × B = 0), so the beam is unaffected.

vivaVoce.question: What is the path of an electron in a uniform electric field?
vivaVoce.answer: Parabolic — analogous to a projectile under gravity.

vivaVoce.question: What is the path of an electron in a uniform magnetic field?
vivaVoce.answer: Circular if entering perpendicular; helical if entering at any other angle.

vivaVoce.question: How is electron mass determined from this experiment?
vivaVoce.answer: By dividing the charge e (from Millikan's oil drop experiment) by the e/m value obtained here.

vivaVoce.question: What are cathode rays?
vivaVoce.answer: Streams of electrons emitted from the cathode of a gas discharge tube at very low pressure.

vivaVoce.question: What is the principle used in Thomson's method?
vivaVoce.answer: Balancing the electric and magnetic forces acting on a moving electron perpendicular to each other to determine its velocity and hence e/m.

vivaVoce.question: Why are bar magnets used in this experiment?
vivaVoce.answer: To produce a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to the electron beam which balances the electric deflection.

title: Charging and Discharging of a Capacitor — Determination of Time Constant
objective: To study the charging and discharging of a capacitor and to find out the time constant.
materials: Charging and discharging kit (RC circuit board)
theory:
The time constant of an RC circuit is given by:

τ = RC

Where:
R = Series resistance (ohms)
C = Capacitance (farads)
τ = Time constant (seconds)

Charging current: I = I₀(1 − e^(−t/RC))
Discharging current: I = I₀ × e^(−t/RC)
Maximum current: I₀ = V₀/R

At t = τ:
Charging current = 0.632 × I₀
Discharging current = 0.368 × I₀

Derivation basis:
Applying Kirchhoff's law: V₀ = IR + Q/C
Since I = dQ/dt, solving gives Q(t) = CV₀(1 − e^(−t/RC))
Differentiating gives the current expressions above.

procedure.instruction: Make the circuit as shown. Note values of R = 10 kΩ, C = 100 µF.
Compute I₀ = V₀/R = 9/10000 = 0.9 mA.
Compute theoretical τ = RC = 10000 × 100×10⁻⁶ = 1.0 s.
Close the switch to start charging. Start the stopwatch simultaneously.
Record ammeter reading at t = 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5 s during charging.
Open the switch to start discharging. Record ammeter readings at the same time intervals.
Plot I vs t for both charging and discharging on the same graph.
From graph: read τ at I = 0.632 × I₀ (charging) and at I = 0.368 × I₀ (discharging).

precautions.instruction: The capacitor must not be leaky; replace if it fails to hold charge.
Start the stopwatch precisely when the switch is opened or closed.
All connections must be tight to avoid resistance variation mid-experiment.
Allow the capacitor to fully discharge before starting the next charging cycle.

defaultResult.summary:
Calculated Time Constant (from graph): 1.0 s
Theoretical Value: τ = RC = 1.0 s

Percentage Error: ((standard - calculated) / standard) × 100 = 0 %
defaultResult.calculation:
Steps:
1. Compute I₀ = V₀/R = 9/10000 = 0.9 mA.
2. From graph, locate the time at which charging current = 0.632 × 0.9 = 0.569 mA → τ = 1.0 s.
3. Similarly, locate the time at which discharging current = 0.368 × 0.9 = 0.331 mA → τ = 1.0 s.
4. Theoretical τ = RC = 10000 × 100×10⁻⁶ = 1.0 s.
5. Both experimental and theoretical values match.

vivaVoce.question: At t = 0, what are I and V across the capacitor when connected to V₀ through R?
vivaVoce.answer: I = V₀/R (maximum) and V = 0 (capacitor is uncharged initially).

vivaVoce.question: What is the time constant for an RC circuit?
vivaVoce.answer: τ = RC — the time for charging current to reach 63.2% of I₀, or for discharging current to fall to 36.8% of I₀.

vivaVoce.question: What is the potential difference across the resistance when the capacitor is fully charged?
vivaVoce.answer: Zero — all supply voltage appears across the capacitor; no current flows.

vivaVoce.question: Does increasing R increase or decrease charging time?
vivaVoce.answer: Increases it — τ = RC grows, so the capacitor charges more slowly with the same C.

vivaVoce.question: After how many time constants is a capacitor considered fully charged?
vivaVoce.answer: After approximately 5τ — at that point the capacitor is charged to over 99% of V₀.

vivaVoce.question: What is a capacitor?
vivaVoce.answer: A device that stores electrical energy in an electric field between two conductors separated by an insulator (dielectric).

vivaVoce.question: What is capacitance?
vivaVoce.answer: The ratio of charge stored to the potential difference across a capacitor, measured in farads (F).

vivaVoce.question: Why does the current decay exponentially?
vivaVoce.answer: Because the rate of charge flow depends on the difference between supply and capacitor voltage, which itself decreases exponentially with time.

vivaVoce.question: What happens if R is replaced by a wire (R = 0)?
vivaVoce.answer: τ becomes zero and the capacitor charges instantaneously, which can damage the capacitor and source due to excessive current.

title: Reverse Characteristics of Zener Diode and Determination of Breakdown Voltage
objective: To draw the reverse characteristic curve of a Zener diode and to find its reverse breakdown voltage.
materials: Zener Diode Kit (Zener diode: 5.6 V rated)

asciiDiagrams.label: Experimental Setup
asciiDiagrams.art:
Rs = 1 kΩ
+---[/\/\/]----------+---( mA )---+
|                    |             |
|                  --+--           |
[E = 12V]          Zener           |
|                  (reverse)       |
|   Rh (rheostat)  --+--           |
|    /\/\/\/          |             |
|                    +-----(V)-----+
|                    |  voltmeter  |
+--------------------+             |
+-----------------------------------+

Zener diode connected in reverse bias with series resistor and variable rheostat.

asciiDiagrams.label: Graph
asciiDiagrams.art:
Reverse Current (mA)
  <----  (−ve Y axis)
  18.00 |. . . . . . . . . . . .___
  12.00 |. . . . . . . . . . ../
   5.00 |. . . . . . . . . ./
   0.50 |. . . . . . . . ./
   0.15 |. . . . . . . . /
   0.08 |. . . . . . . ./
   0.00 +_______________/____________
         0  1  2  3  4  5 5.3 5.6 5.8 5.9
                               ^
                              Vz = 5.6 V (Breakdown)
                              Reverse Voltage (V) →

Plot reverse current vs reverse voltage. The sharp knee in the curve gives the breakdown voltage Vz.

theory:
A Zener diode operates in the reverse breakdown region where voltage remains nearly constant regardless of current. The breakdown voltage Vz is determined graphically from the knee of the reverse I–V characteristic.

At breakdown:
ΔI is very large while ΔV ≈ 0

Breakdown Mechanisms:
1. Zener breakdown: Occurs in heavily doped junctions where the electric field directly ruptures covalent bonds. Dominates for Vz < 6V.
2. Avalanche breakdown: Occurs in lightly doped junctions where accelerated carriers cause impact ionization. Dominates for Vz > 6V.

Temperature Effects:
- Zener breakdown voltage DECREASES with temperature.
- Avalanche breakdown voltage INCREASES with temperature.

Below breakdown: Only a small leakage current flows (~μA to mA).
At breakdown: Current rises sharply while voltage stays constant at Vz.

procedure.instruction: Make connections as shown: Zener in reverse bias with Rs in series and rheostat for voltage control.
Set rheostat to minimum. Switch on supply. Note zero readings on voltmeter and milliammeter.
Slowly increase voltage in steps of ~0.5 V. At each step note voltmeter (V) and milliammeter (mA) readings.
Continue until the milliammeter shows a sharp rise while voltmeter reading remains approximately constant — this is the breakdown region.
Reduce step size to 0.1 V near the knee for better resolution.
Plot reverse current (mA) on −ve Y-axis vs reverse voltage (V) on −ve X-axis.
Read Vz from the graph as the voltage at the knee (sharp current rise).

precautions.instruction: Do not exceed power rating of the Zener; use Rs to limit current.
Increase voltage slowly in small steps to capture the knee accurately.
Check and record zero errors of both meters before starting.
Ensure Zener is in reverse bias — verify polarity before switching on.

defaultResult.summary:
Calculated Breakdown Voltage Vz: 5.6 V
Standard (Rated) Value: 5.6 V

Percentage Error: ((standard - calculated) / standard) × 100 = 0 %
defaultResult.calculation:
Steps:
1. Plot reverse current (mA) vs reverse voltage (V).
2. Identify the knee of the curve where current rises sharply.
3. From the observation table, current jumps from 0.50 mA to 5.00 mA between 5.3 V and 5.6 V.
4. The breakdown voltage Vz is read at the knee = 5.6 V.
5. This matches the rated value of the Zener diode.

vivaVoce.question: What is a Zener diode?
vivaVoce.answer: A specially doped p-n junction diode designed to operate safely in the reverse breakdown region, used mainly for voltage regulation.

vivaVoce.question: What are the two types of reverse breakdown?
vivaVoce.answer: (i) Zener breakdown — in heavily doped thin-depletion junctions; strong field directly ruptures covalent bonds. (ii) Avalanche breakdown — in lightly doped junctions; carriers multiply by impact ionization.

vivaVoce.question: How does Zener breakdown voltage vary with temperature?
vivaVoce.answer: It DECREASES — higher temperature gives valence electrons more energy, requiring less applied voltage to break bonds.

vivaVoce.question: How does avalanche breakdown voltage vary with temperature?
vivaVoce.answer: It INCREASES — more lattice collisions require a higher field to sustain carrier multiplication.

vivaVoce.question: Why is the Zener diode used as a voltage regulator?
vivaVoce.answer: In the breakdown region, large current changes cause negligible voltage change — Vz stays nearly constant, stabilising the output.

vivaVoce.question: What differentiates a Zener diode from a conventional rectifier diode?
vivaVoce.answer: A Zener is designed and rated to operate safely in the reverse breakdown region; conventional diodes are destroyed if operated there.

vivaVoce.question: What is the symbol of a Zener diode?
vivaVoce.answer: Similar to a normal diode but with Z-shaped (bent) cathode bar.

vivaVoce.question: What is the knee voltage?
vivaVoce.answer: The reverse voltage at which the Zener diode begins to conduct heavily — also called the Zener breakdown voltage Vz.

vivaVoce.question: Why is a series resistor used with a Zener diode?
vivaVoce.answer: To limit the current through the diode and prevent it from exceeding its power rating during breakdown.

title: Study of Hall Effect — Hall Coefficient, Carrier Density and Mobility
objective: To study the Hall Effect and determine the Hall coefficient, carrier density and mobility of a given semiconductor material.

asciiDiagrams.label: Experimental Setup
asciiDiagrams.art:
I_x  (current, X-direction)
               |
               V
        +------+------+
        |              |
Bz ↑   |  n-Ge slab   | ----> Ey (Hall voltage, Y-direction)
        |              |       [millivoltmeter]
        +--------------+
              t (thickness)

  Bz = magnetic field (perpendicular, Z-direction)
  Ix = current along X-axis (controlled by rheostat)
  Ey = Hall voltage measured across Y-faces

  Lorentz force deflects carriers → charge accumulates → Hall voltage Ey develops

theory:
The Hall coefficient, carrier density, and mobility are determined from the Hall effect:

RH = (Ey × t) / (Ix × Bz)   m³/Coulomb

n = 1 / (e × RH)   per m³

μ = RH × σ   m²/V·s

Where:
Ey = Hall voltage across Y-faces of specimen (volts)
Ix = Current through specimen along X-axis (amperes)
t = Thickness of semiconductor slab (metres)
Bz = Magnetic field perpendicular to slab (Wb/m²)
n = Carrier concentration (per m³)
e = Electron charge = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C
σ = Electrical conductivity of sample (S/m)

Derivation basis:
Magnetic force F_B = e × v_d × Bz
At equilibrium: e × Ey = e × v_d × Bz
Current density J_x = n × e × v_d
Combining gives RH = 1/(n × e)

Sign convention: RH negative → n-type (electrons); RH positive → p-type (holes).

procedure.instruction: Measure thickness t of semiconductor slab in metres. Note Bz from Gauss meter.
Place the slab in the electromagnet gap and connect as shown. Ensure Bz is perpendicular to slab surface.
Pass current Ix = 2 mA through slab along X-axis using rheostat.
Measure Hall voltage Ey with millivoltmeter.
Repeat for Ix = 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 mA and note corresponding Ey values.
Plot Ey vs Ix. Compute slope = BC/AC from graph.
Compute RH = slope × (t / Bz).
Compute carrier density n = 1/(e × RH) and mobility μ = RH × σ.

precautions.instruction: Keep the electromagnet air-gap constant throughout — any change alters Bz.
Keep current within permissible limits for the semiconductor sample to avoid heating.
Calibrate Gauss meter before the experiment.
Use millivoltmeter with high input impedance for accurate Ey measurement.
Maintain constant Bz for each set of Ix readings.

defaultResult.summary:
Calculated Hall Coefficient RH: 0.0125 m³/C
Calculated Carrier Density n: 5.0 × 10²⁰ per m³
Calculated Mobility μ: 0.03125 m²/V·s

Material Type: n-type Germanium (confirmed by sign of RH)

vivaVoce.question: What is the Hall effect?
vivaVoce.answer: When a current-carrying conductor is placed in a perpendicular magnetic field, a transverse voltage (Hall voltage) develops across it due to Lorentz force deflecting charge carriers.

vivaVoce.question: What does the sign of RH tell us?
vivaVoce.answer: RH negative → n-type (electrons); RH positive → p-type (holes).

vivaVoce.question: What is the order of Hall voltage?
vivaVoce.answer: A few millivolts, depending on current and magnetic field strength.

vivaVoce.question: Define carrier mobility.
vivaVoce.answer: Drift velocity per unit applied electric field — μ = v_d/E (m²/V·s or cm²/V·s).

vivaVoce.question: What are the applications of Hall effect?
vivaVoce.answer: (i) Identify carrier type; (ii) measure carrier concentration; (iii) measure magnetic field (Hall probe); (iv) determine carrier mobility.

vivaVoce.question: What is a Hall probe?
vivaVoce.answer: A device based on Hall effect used to measure magnetic field strength.

vivaVoce.question: Why does Hall voltage develop?
vivaVoce.answer: Due to the Lorentz force (F = ev × B) on moving charge carriers, which deflects them to one face of the specimen, creating charge accumulation and a transverse electric field.

vivaVoce.question: What is carrier concentration?
vivaVoce.answer: The number of charge carriers (electrons or holes) per unit volume of the semiconductor.

vivaVoce.question: What is the Lorentz force?
vivaVoce.answer: The total electromagnetic force on a charged particle: F = qE + qv × B.

vivaVoce.question: Why must the magnetic field be perpendicular to the current?
vivaVoce.answer: To produce maximum Lorentz force on the carriers perpendicular to both current and field, generating maximum Hall voltage.

title: Determination of Frequency of Tuning Fork by Melde's Method
objective: To determine the frequency of an electrically maintained tuning fork by means of Melde's apparatus in longitudinal and transverse modes of vibration.
materials: Step-down transformer (230V AC to low voltage)

asciiDiagrams.label: Experimental Setup
asciiDiagrams.art:
TRANSVERSE MODE (String parallel to prong):
   [Fork F]  P1          P2          P3     Pulley A
    |_|     |            |            |         /\
    | |     |            |            |        /  \
   [F]~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~/    \
               (stationary loops)                  [Pan+Weights]
   Frequency: v = (1/2l) × √(Mg/m)

LONGITUDINAL MODE (String perpendicular to prong):
   [Fork F]  P1    P2    P3    P4    P5   Pulley A
    |_|      |     |     |     |     |       /\
    | |      |     |     |     |     |      /  \
   [F]================================================
                (loops in transverse plane)    [Pan+Weights]
   Frequency: v = (1/l) × √(Mg/m)

asciiDiagrams.label: Graph
asciiDiagrams.art:
l² (cm²)
  ^
  |                          * (Longitudinal)
  |                       *
  |                    *
  |                 *
  |              *
  |           *     * (Transverse)
  |        *     *
  |     *     *
  |  *     *
  +__________________________________> M (gm)
       10    15    20    25

Plot graph of l² vs M. Straight line through origin. Slope used to verify frequency.

theory:
The frequency of the tuning fork is determined using Melde's method in two modes:

Transverse mode: v = (1/2l) × √(Mg/m)
Longitudinal mode: v = (1/l) × √(Mg/m)

Where:
v = Frequency of tuning fork (Hz)
l = Length of one loop = L/p (cm)
L = Total length of vibrating thread (cm)
p = Number of loops formed
M = Total mass = pan + added weights (gm)
m = Mass per unit length of thread (gm/cm)
g = Acceleration due to gravity = 980 cm/s²

Key Points:
- In transverse mode, the string vibrates at half the fork frequency.
- In longitudinal mode, the string vibrates at the full fork frequency.
- Therefore, for the same load, longitudinal loop length is approximately double the transverse loop length.
- Total mass M must include pan mass (5 gm) plus added weights.

procedure.instruction: Connect step-down transformer primary to AC mains. Connect secondary to tuning fork terminals.
Tie thread to prong. Pass over pulley. Attach scale pan. Measure pan mass mP = 5 gm.
Arrange fork in TRANSVERSE mode (string parallel to prong). Load 5 gm on pan (total M = 10 gm). Excite fork.
Adjust thread length until stable horizontal loops form. Measure total length L, count loops p. Compute l = L/p.
Increase load to 10, 15, 20 gm and repeat Step 4 for each (total M = 15, 20, 25 gm).
Rearrange fork in LONGITUDINAL mode (string perpendicular to prong). Load 5 gm. Excite fork.
Adjust thread length until stable transverse-plane loops form. Measure L, p, compute l.
Repeat for loads 10, 15, 20 gm in longitudinal mode.
Measure mT (mass of 10 m of thread). Compute m = mT/10 gm/cm.
Substitute l, M, m, g into appropriate equation for each row. Compute v. Take mean.

precautions.instruction: Minimise pulley friction — apply a small drop of oil if necessary.
Thread must be thin, uniform and flexible — non-uniform thread gives irregular loop lengths.
Always include pan mass (5 gm) in total mass M.
Ensure the stationary wave pattern is stable before measuring L.
Do not touch the vibrating prong while the fork is energised.

defaultResult.summary:
Calculated Mean Frequency (Transverse): 99.45 Hz
Calculated Mean Frequency (Longitudinal): 99.48 Hz
Overall Mean Frequency: 99.46 Hz
Standard Value: 100 Hz

Percentage Error: ((standard - calculated) / standard) × 100 = 0.54 %

vivaVoce.question: What do you mean by an electrically maintained tuning fork?
vivaVoce.answer: A fork whose vibration is sustained by AC current — an electromagnetic coil attracts the magnetic prong on each half-cycle, maintaining continuous oscillation.

vivaVoce.question: What should be the frequency of a fork maintained by 50 Hz AC?
vivaVoce.answer: 100 Hz — the fork receives two electromagnetic impulses per AC cycle.

vivaVoce.question: What type of waves are produced in the thread?
vivaVoce.answer: Stationary (standing) waves formed by superposition of incident and reflected waves.

vivaVoce.question: Why is the longitudinal loop length double the transverse loop length?
vivaVoce.answer: In longitudinal mode, fork frequency = v_wave/l, while in transverse mode, fork frequency = v_wave/(2l). For the same frequency, l_longitudinal = 2 × l_transverse.

vivaVoce.question: Why must the thread be thin and uniform?
vivaVoce.answer: To ensure constant mass per unit length m throughout — a non-uniform thread produces irregular loop sizes and inaccurate frequency calculation.

vivaVoce.question: Can you pass a pencil through the loops?
vivaVoce.answer: No — the thread is vibrating so rapidly (100 times/sec) that it only appears to form loops; it is always in motion.

vivaVoce.question: What are nodes and antinodes?
vivaVoce.answer: Nodes are points of zero displacement in a stationary wave; antinodes are points of maximum displacement.

vivaVoce.question: What is the principle of Melde's experiment?
vivaVoce.answer: Formation of stationary waves in a stretched string due to interference of incident and reflected waves when the string length is an integer multiple of the loop length.

vivaVoce.question: What is the effect of increasing tension on loop length?
vivaVoce.answer: Loop length increases with tension since wave speed v = √(T/m) increases, and for a fixed frequency, l ∝ v.

vivaVoce.question: What are the applications of Melde's method?
vivaVoce.answer: (i) Determine frequency of tuning fork; (ii) determine mass per unit length of string; (iii) study stationary waves.
